headline: Young People Prove Anxiety Doesn't Stop Action
meta_description: Despite daily worries, young people across the UK are taking concrete steps to build a kinder world, from tree planting to community volunteering.
summary_short: New research shows young people feel anxious about the future—yet 97% believe they can make a positive difference, and many already are.
body:
A nine-year-old named Vidhi raised more than £6,000 for a children's hospital charity. Elsewhere, young people described planting flowers at school, collecting litter and coaching younger children. These aren't isolated acts of kindness—they're part of a wider pattern emerging across UK youth research.

New surveys reveal a striking paradox: while 53% of 16 to 25-year-olds feel anxious about their future daily, 70% are determined to achieve their goals. Even more tellingly, 97% of young readers surveyed by The Week Junior said they felt able to make a positive difference in the world.

The evidence suggests young people aren't paralyzed by worry. Instead, they're channeling concern into action. In one study, 73% of 16 to 29-year-olds had participated in at least one political or community activity during the previous year. When asked what would make the world better, they pointed to climate action, kindness, equality and ending wars.

Perhaps most revealing: 59% of young people surveyed already belong to community groups—Scouts, sports teams, volunteering projects—while 58% of those who don't said they would like to join one.

Young people may be anxious, but their vision for change is clear, and their commitment is real.
